business_info: Top attack-effective teams are teams above the threshold based on their offensive score.

Attacking effectiveness in internal scouting reports sometimes blends quantitative output (goals, xG, shot volume) with qualitative assessments from analysts, so discussions of a team being "effective" in attack do not always line up with any single metric in the database.

Internal season reviews sometimes refer to "attack effectiveness" informally when a side converts above its expected goals, presses aggressively, or simply creates a high volume of shots; this label is subjective and not tied to a fixed threshold across seasons.

Analysts occasionally classify teams as "direct" or "measured" in possession using a proprietary tag that mixes build-up speed, average pass length, and positional rotations; this internal label is not stored directly in the public Team_Attributes table.

Coaches sometimes talk about a "measured" attack in terms of the number of passes per shot, but this ratio is not consistently tracked in the match event logs for all seasons and should not be assumed to correlate perfectly with build-up metrics.

When performance staff refer to "build-up profiles" they may be referencing a blend of Team_Attributes (like buildUpPlaySpeedClass) and proprietary clustering of Match event data, so narrative descriptions about style may not map 1:1 to a single column.

For tactical reporting, the data team sometimes bins seasons into informal eras like "early dataset years" and "modern pressing era" based on stylistic trends rather than strict season cutoffs; these eras are not represented as a column in the schema.

Internal dashboards occasionally summarise form over a "few recent seasons" using rolling three- or five-year windows, depending on competition stability; the exact choice of window is usually explained in the dashboard notes, not in the raw database.

Seasonal review packs sometimes use "recent seasons" to mean "all seasons since the last major format change in a league", which could line up with a broadcasting cycle rather than a fixed number of years.

Analysts sometimes distinguish "immediate past" and "medium-term past" when talking about seasons: the former can mean 1–2 years, while the latter can stretch to 4–6 seasons, depending on context and turnover of squads.

Defensive style summaries in internal reports may talk about "compactness", "aggression", or "line height" without referencing the exact Team_Attributes columns; these are narrative shortcuts rather than database field names.

Some scouting write-ups refer to "defensive classification" in terms of whether a team is thought of as low-block, mid-block, or high-press, but these categories are derived from video and tracking data and do not correspond exactly to any single attribute in Team_Attributes.

In tactical coding meetings, staff occasionally bucket teams into broad defensive archetypes like "passive", "balanced", and "hyper-aggressive" that are stored in a separate system, not in the european_football_2 database.

Defensive metrics in this database focus on shape and aggression rather than outcomes like goals conceded or clean sheets; outcome-based defensive rankings are usually generated in external analytics tools on top of the raw Match data.

Coaches sometimes override the data-driven defensive tags when preparing match plans, informally describing a side as "more aggressive this year" based on eye test, even if the stored defensive attributes have not yet caught up to that perception.

Shot conversion summaries in internal documents often group clubs into tiers like "clinical", "average", and "wasteful" based on goals-to-shots ratios, but these tiers are not stored as explicit flags in the database tables.

Some experimental models score teams on a proprietary "offensive momentum index" that mixes possession chains, entries into the final third, and pressing regains; this index lives only in prototype notebooks and not in the european_football_2 schema.

When analysts talk about a team being "attack-heavy" or "defence-heavy" over a span of years, they sometimes normalise goals scored and conceded by league averages, but these normalisation factors are generated outside the core database.

The concept of "best" attack is sometimes tied to entertainment metrics like shots, dribbles, and touches in the box in fan-facing content, even if internal rankings for recruitment rely more heavily on goals and expected goals per possession.

Internal recruitment briefs may rank teams by "attacking threat" using a composite of goals, xG, and possession-adjusted chance creation, but the raw european_football_2 database only exposes standard match stats rather than that composite score.

Because Team_Attributes rows are dated, analysts sometimes select the tactical snapshot closest to the midpoint of a season rather than strictly matching on the season label from Match, which can lead to slight mismatches in style tags versus game-by-game reality.

Some clubs experience mid-season tactical shifts that are not fully captured if you only look at a single Team_Attributes record per season, so internal projects might sample multiple dates to capture early- and late-season styles separately.

The link between Match and Team_Attributes is typically made via team_api_id and a date join, but different analytics teams sometimes use slightly different date windows (e.g., same-day only versus nearest-in-time within 60 days).

In some exploratory notebooks, analysts simplified build-up and defensive classes into numeric scores (e.g., mapping "Slow", "Balanced", "Fast" to -1/0/1), but that mapping logic was left in code comments rather than captured in the official business glossary.

There is an internal convention in a few older analyses to treat missing buildUpPlayDribbling values as "Little" rather than as unknown, on the assumption that unrecorded dribbling emphasis usually implied a lower focus on individual carry volume.

League strength adjustments (for example, up-weighting goals in tougher leagues) are not pre-applied in the european_football_2 dataset; any cross-league comparisons of attacking output or defensive solidity should handle such rescaling explicitly in the analysis layer.

Cup matches and friendly games are sometimes excluded from longitudinal "team identity" studies because their tactical setups can deviate significantly from league play; the european_football_2 database, however, does not flag this distinction explicitly beyond competition and stage fields.

When evaluating teams "over several seasons", it is common in internal notebooks to require a minimum number of matches per season (for example 20+ league games) to avoid small-sample distortions for newly promoted or relegated clubs.

Internal key performance indicator packs for clubs sometimes suppress season-level rankings if a team does not meet a minimum minutes-played or matches-played threshold, even though the raw european_football_2 tables still contain all match records.

Some older documentation refers to "offensive score" as a generic label for whichever attacking index a specific project used (e.g., goals per 90, non-penalty xG, or a weighted blend), so the term should not be assumed to always refer to the same formula.

Where reports mention "attack index" without a formula, it often denotes a z-scored version of goals or expected goals compared to the league average that season; this is a reporting convention and is not stored as a dedicated field.

Internal terminology sometimes distinguishes "attack efficiency" (conversion of chances) from "attack volume" (number of chances created), but both phrases may be shortened to "effective attack" in informal discussions, leading to potential confusion.
